coredata.dc:title: AND logic gate supported novel speckled phosphorus-doped carbon dots decorated ZrO2/CaO/MgO sonocatalysts for efficient MB dye decolorization
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Kritika S.
Mudgal, Anurag
Nair, Manjula
Kumar, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description:
Textile dyes are significant contributors to aquatic pollution in developing nations, and also the current pace of human-caused contamination of worldwide freshwater bodies is frightening. Herein, we report the sonocatalytic decolorization of the methylene blue (MB) dye using a novel synthesis of composite materials of zirconium oxide (ZrO2), calcium oxide (CaO), and magnesium oxide (MgO) nanoparticles (NPs) decorated by phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs). The monoclinic phase of ZrO2 has shown the highest catalytic activity, thus imparting greater decolorization efficiency (DE) in the synthesized metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) decorated with PCDs (MONPs@PCDs). The sonocatalytic efficiency of MONPs@PCDs was evaluated using MB as the analyte molecule. We report DE of up to 99.54% via speckled PCDs decoration and synergistic effect in a short time of 30 min at just 20 kHz frequency. The sonocatalytic activity of MONPs@PCDs is AND logic gate supported and was found as ZCMO@PCDs (99.54%) > ZMO@PCDs (96.47%) > ZO@PCDs (94.68%) > ZCO@PCDs (93.40%). However, the sonocatalytic activity of MONPs was only ZCMO (11.21%) > ZMO (9.26%) > ZO (3.79%) > ZCO (2.92%), under identical experimental conditions. The synergistic effect in polymetallic systems opens new pathways to be explored in sonocatalysis. We confirmed the synthesis of PCDs and their speckled decoration on the surface of MONPs using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The speckled nanocomposites, i.e., MONPs@PCDs, showed an enhanced and faster decolorization efficiency than the bare MONPs, as demonstrated by the kinetics studies as 101 fold increase in k values and 101 fold decrease in t1/2 values.
Novelty statement Novel nanocomposites have been developed for MB dye decolorization using a low frequency of just 20 kHz and possessing 99.54–93.40% DE in only 30 min, thus may aid in industrial dye decolorization and the reuse of wastewater for environmental sustainability.

Sonocatalysis
Decolorization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Textile dyes are significant contributors to aquatic pollution in developing nations, and also the current pace of human-caused contamination of worldwide freshwater bodies is frightening. Herein, we report the sonocatalytic decolorization of the methylene blue (MB) dye using a novel synthesis of composite materials of zirconium oxide (ZrO2), calcium oxide (CaO), and magnesium oxide (MgO) nanoparticles (NPs) decorated by phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs). The monoclinic phase of ZrO2 has shown the highest catalytic activity, thus imparting greater decolorization efficiency (DE) in the synthesized metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) decorated with PCDs (MONPs@PCDs). The sonocatalytic efficiency of MONPs@PCDs was evaluated using MB as the analyte molecule. We report DE of up to 99.54% via speckled PCDs decoration and synergistic effect in a short time of 30 min at just 20 kHz frequency. The sonocatalytic activity of MONPs@PCDs is AND logic gate supported and was found as ZCMO@PCDs (99.54%) > ZMO@PCDs (96.47%) > ZO@PCDs (94.68%) > ZCO@PCDs (93.40%). However, the sonocatalytic activity of MONPs was only ZCMO (11.21%) > ZMO (9.26%) > ZO (3.79%) > ZCO (2.92%), under identical experimental conditions. The synergistic effect in polymetallic systems opens new pathways to be explored in sonocatalysis. We confirmed the synthesis of PCDs and their speckled decoration on the surface of MONPs using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The speckled nanocomposites, i.e., MONPs@PCDs, showed an enhanced and faster decolorization efficiency than the bare MONPs, as demonstrated by the kinetics studies as 101 fold increase in k values and 101 fold decrease in t1/2 values.

Novel nanocomposites have been developed for MB dye decolorization using a low frequency of just 20 kHz and possessing 99.54–93.40% DE in only 30 min, thus may aid in industrial dye decolorization and the reuse of wastewater for environmental sustainability.

ce:section.ce:para: The modern textile business has led to innovative, avant-garde fabric manufacturing and processing technologies. Dying is one of those operations that might produce the most ecologically harmful waste effluent among all the procedures required in fabric production [1]. Also, some bioaccumulative textile dyes are suspected of causing mutagenicity and genotoxicity [2]. Methylene blue (MB) causes adverse health effects such as mild bladder discomfort, headaches, increased perspiration, nausea [3], vomiting, abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, stomachache [4], increased urination, abdominal cramps, dizziness [5], etc. Among these, chest pain and confusion are common side effects of high dosage of MB [6]. Furthermore, MB has the potential to cause cancer [7]. MB inhibits monoamine oxidase, produces potentially lethal serotonin poisoning at doses greater than 5 mg/kg, and causes severe anaphylactic shock in rare cases [8]. Because of the adverse health effects of discharged organic dyes, many treatment technologies for colored effluents have been developed, including membrane technology [9], biological processes [10], electrochemical processes [11], and advanced oxidation processes [12], etc.

Sonocatalysis is a promising field with many commercial applications among these methods. It has piqued the curiosity of scientists due to its high efficiency, low maintenance, low energy efficiency, and ease of use [13].

Unlike UV light, ultrasound (US) waves can pass through opaque systems completely. As a result, unlike many other processes, the efficiency of US processes is not hampered with increase in the amount of turbidity or suspended solid. Also, because of its unique ability to create hydroxyl (•OH) radicals in bulk solution, the use of US has garnered a lot of attention. Using US in the presence of an appropriate sonocatalyst, known as sonocatalysis, has recently attracted significant interest in degrading or decolorizing organic dyes in the aqueous phase [14]. However, total mineralization of the target contaminants by sonolysis, on the other hand, involves a substantial amount of energy and a longer reaction duration. To alleviate this difficulty, ultrasonic irradiation in the presence of a suitable semiconductor as a sonocatalyst can be used to speed up the reaction. The usage of metal oxide-based semiconductors as sonocatalysts has been proven successful in decolorizing dyes in aqueous systems [15].

Nanoparticles (NPs) and their nanocomposites of various metal oxides, including WO3, ZnO, TiO2, MgO, CaO, and ZrO2, etc. [16,17], have been found to possess catalytic activity. Although several materials have been tested for sonocatalytic activity for dye degradation, ZrO2, CaO, and MgO are chemically stable and inexpensive oxides, widely used today as high-performance engineering materials [18]. Because of the large bandgap of ZrO2, CaO, and MgO, excited electrons (e–) can quickly recombine with holes (h+), lowering total catalytic efficiency and limiting their application in sonocatalysis [19–21]. To solve this problem, methods such as doping [22], including noble metals into the semiconductor matrix [23], coupling with semiconductors having a smaller bandgap [24], and organic dye-induced sensitization [25] have been used.

Recently, novel approaches for enhancing the catalytic effectiveness of semiconductor materials by integrating carbon-containing compounds such as carbon quantum dots (CDs) [22], graphene [26], fullerenes [27] solely, and carbon nanotubes [28] have been developed.

Due to their distinct behavior and properties, quantum dots (QDs) are a special type of nanomaterial of 2–10 nm in size [29]. CDs are a new form of QD [22] comparable in size and constructed entirely of carbon-containing or carbonaceous compounds. CDs' suitability for catalytic use in their free form or integrated with semiconductor NPs is also being explored. Compared to other carbonaceous compounds, CDs are superior due to their simple synthesis method and can be made in an environmentally benign, inexpensive, and with greater yield [30]. The conjugated structure of the CDs is thought to be responsible for the increased sonocatalytic activity since it facilitates the adsorption of organic molecules on them, allowing them to be attacked by hydroxyl (•OH) and oxygen (O•) radicals. In these cases, a significant interaction between the semiconductor NPs and the CDs is thought to stabilize the composite and aid the charge transfer process, resulting in improved sonocatalytic activity [22]. Doping of heteroatoms is an appealing method of modifying the electronic density and tuning the intrinsic characteristics of CDs [31]. The involvement of n→π* transition in conjugated C = X bonds (X is P) of phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs) results in a dopant state near the CDs' lowest unoccupied molecular orbital level [32]. Carbon materials doped with heteroatoms with bigger (P, S) or smaller (N) diameters than carbon have been shown to have noticeable activity [33,34]. Graphene [35], microporous carbon [36], and co-doped graphene are some of the N and S-doped carbon-based materials that have been reported [37].

PCDs can mediate e– transport while preventing recombination between h+ and e−. Because of the surface attached PCDs, recombination of e– and h+ in metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) has been greatly decreased [38,39]. However, PCDs for dye decolorization have received very little attention so far. Customarily reported studies exhibit more time and energy or frequency consuming decolrization results. Speckled decoration of PCDs shows more catalytic activity than regular distribution due to increased surface area; thus, possesses more active sites and faster decolorization results were obtained.

Present work presents decolorization efficiency up to 99.54% using MONPs with speckled decoration of PCDs (MONPs@PCDs) in mere 30 min at just 20 kHz frequency. We have used a simple one-step hydrothermal approach for adsorbing PCDs on the surface of ZrO2, CaO, and MgO NPs to create highly effective sonocatalytic composites. The recombination of e– and h+ in many catalyst materials is a serious issue that reduces the catalyst's efficiency. This challenge was solved in our instance by adsorbing PCDs on the surface of MONPs. To the best of our knowledge, no attempts have been made until now to modify ZrO2, CaO, and MgO and study their synergistic effect with phosphorous doped carbon-containing compounds to construct an effective sonocatalyst at a low frequency of 20 kHz for MB decolorization, for sustainable wastewater reuse.
All chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade and were procured from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India. All the solutions were prepared in Millipore water.

For preparing PCDs, 1 g of dextrose and 0.1 g of Na2HPO4 were dissolved in 25 ml of Millipore water in the round bottom flask [34]. The solution was refluxed at 200 °C for 1 h until the formation of a dark-brown solution. The prepared solution was passed through the syringe filter (0.1 μm) to remove the large aggregates. For comparison purpose CDs were prepared [40].

The ZrO2, CaO, and MgO NPs were synthesized by the sol-gel method, and stoichiometric amount of ZrOCl2.8H2O were dissolved in a mixture of water and ethanol to prepare the zirconia sol. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 RPM for 30 min and sonicated for 30 min. The pH of the solution was monitored and kept under one initially. 0.1 M ammonium hydroxide solution was added dropwise to the mixture until the formation of colloids and the pH reached ∼10. The colloidal precipitate was washed well with millipore water and calcined at 800 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace. This product will be denoted as ZO hereafter.

Similarly, zirconium-calcium-magnesium oxide (ZCMO), zirconium-calcium oxide (ZCO), and zirconium-magnesium oxide (ZMO) were prepared by the same method with ZrOCl2.8H2O + CaCl2·2H2O + MgCl2·6H2O, ZrOCl2.8H2O + CaCl2·2H2O and ZrOCl2.8H2O + MgCl2·6H2O, respectively to study the synergistic effect.

As-synthesized ZO was decorated with PCDs via simple one-step hydrothermal method. For this, 1 g ZO and 40 ml of PCDs were transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in an oven (180 °C for 6 h). Similarly, ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs were prepared by the same method and then were washed several times with water and ethanol and dried till constant weight was obtained.

The sonocatalytic activity of ZO, ZCMO, ZCO, ZMO, and ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, ZMO@PCDs was examined by the destructive decolorization of MB in bulk solution. An ultrasonic bath (SB-300 DTY ultrasonicator) at 20 kHz was used to irradiate the solution containing MB. Simultaneously, it was placed in the dark. The residual concentration of MB was measured spectrophotometrically. The MB decolorization efficiency (DE), was calculated by the following equation:
DE
( % )
=
Co − C
Co
× 100
where Co is the initial concentration of MB, and C is the concentration of MB after sonocatalytic activity.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on UV-Vis spectrophotometer (2060+, Analytical). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed to investigate the formation of MONPs and MONPs@PCDs (SP-65, PerkinElmer). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM 2100 TEM HR LaB6 Version, JEOL) showed decoration of PCDs on the surface of MONPs. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (EVO 18, Carl Zeiss) was used to observe surface morphology, and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) (AXS D8 Focus P-XRD, Bruker) was used to investigate crystallinity and phase identification.

The UV–Vis absorption profile of the synthesized PCDs is shown in Fig. 1 . The peak at 270 nm in the absorption spectrum is typical of colloidal CDs absorption characteristics [39]. The origin of this peak is related to π electron transitions in π* molecular orbitals of C=C bonds [40].

The XRD results confirm that the monoclinic structure of the as-obtained ZrO2, according to the JCPDS 37–1484 (Fig. 2 ) [41], the observed peaks at 17.2°, 22.1°, 24.3°, 25.0°, 28.0°, 31.3°, 34.3°, 35.4°, 38.4°, 40.8°, 44.8°, 49.1°, 50.0°, 53.8°, 55.3°, 60.0°, 62.8° and 65.6° with corresponding [hkl] values [001], [110], [110], [011], [−111], [111], [200], [002], [120], [112], [211], [220], [002], [220], [310], [131], [113] and [222], respectively. Compared to the tetragonal and cubic phases of ZrO2, the monoclinic phase has been shown to have the highest catalytic activity [42], thus, imparting greater DE in the synthesized MONPs@PCDs. Also, the results were matched with the JCPDF card number 77–2376 of the face-centered cubic phase of CaO NPs, the observed peaks at 32.2°, 50.6°, and 62.6° with corresponding [hkl] values [111], [220], and [311], respectively [43]. The observed peaks at 35.2○, 42.8○, 60.1○, 74.8○, and 78.9○ are associated with the simple cubic rock-salt structure of MgO NPs with the [111], [200], [220], [311], and [222], respectively, which agree with a former study [44]. In MONPs@PCDs, the intensities of crystal planes or peaks were relatively lower than those of bare MONPs. These decrease in intensities may be due to the higher adsorption of PCDs on those specific planes.

The sonocatalytic activity of MONPs@PCDs was found as ZCMO@PCDs (99.54%) > ZMO@PCDs (96.47%) > ZO@PCDs (94.68%) > ZCO@PCDs (93.40%). However, the sonocatalytic activity of MONPs was only ZCMO (11.21%) > ZMO (9.26%) > ZO (3.79%) > ZCO (2.92%), under identical experimental conditions.

The greater sonocatalytic activity of ZCMO@PCDs may be attributed to the synergistic effect in polymetallic systems. Polymetallic systems can create a unique structure and a highly open system, resulting in the production and exposure of many active sites [45]. The percentage decolorization of developed sonocatalysts is higher than the reported catalysts, as shown in Table 2 .

According to eq. (1), using US alone yields •OH radicals in a bulk solution containing H2O molecules. However, the US alone is ineffective in generating enough •OH radicals in the solution to decolorize MB significantly [52]. NPs in conjunction with ultrasonic irradiation (sonocatalysis) led to increased DE. The "hot spot" and "sonoluminescence" phenomena can explain the sonocatalysis mechanism in the presence of NPs [53]. Cavitation bubbles are formed, expand, and then collapse during the 'hot spot phenomenon,' resulting in high pressure (>1000 atm) and high temperature (>4000 K), leading to the formation of free oxidizing radicals in the solution, as shown in eqs. (2)–(4) [15].
As-generated hydrogen peroxide can provide specific oxidizing agents for improved decolorization of MB by the solid-phase catalyst, such as MONPs, as shown in eqs. (5)–(8) [54].

Ultrasonic irradiation produces sonoluminescence, which will excite the sonocatalyst surface and create •OH in the liquid phase. As demonstrated in eqs. (9)–(11), the sonocatalyst plays a photocatalytic function during the sonoluminescence phenomena.

The sonoluminescence produced by collapsing cavitation bubbles can enhance the conversion of Mn+1 to Mn in MONPs. This aids in the effective decolorization of MB [55]. Utilizing a solid catalyst with small particles in sonocatalysis offers more nuclei for cavity formation, resulting in more cavitation bubbles in the bulk solution [53]. Because of decoration of MONPs with PCDs, the use of MONPs@PCDs considerably boosted the DE compared to MONPs. Finally, as shown in Fig. 4 , the addition of phosphorous increased the catalytic capabilities of CDs, therefore, PCDs were used to effectively decorate MONPs and boost the catalytic degradation of MB. Inclusion of phosphorous as dopant for CDs resulted in the superior photoluminescence property of PCDs [34], was primarily responsible for the improved DE of MONPs@PCDs.

Because of sonogenerated heat and light, the excited e– in the conduction band (CB) of PCDs might be transferred to the CB of MONPs. Simultaneously, h+ created in the valence band (VB) of MONPs might move to the VB of PCDs. •OH radicals are formed from the reaction of e– in the CB with hydrogen peroxide [56].

Also, the bandgaps of MONPs and MONPs@PCDs were determined to make a realistic comparison. The bandgap energies corresponding to direct and indirect transitions in ZO were 1.364 and 4.586 eV, compared to 1.156 and 4.569 eV in ZO@PCDs; in ZCMO were 3.975 and 5.889 eV, compared to 2.531 and 5.196 eV in ZCMO@PCDs; in ZCO were 3.427 and 5.134 eV, compared to 3.281 and 5.049 eV in ZCO@PCDs; and in ZMO were 5.102 and 5.877 eV, compared to 1.804and 4.248 eV in ZMO@PCDs as shown in Fig. 5 .
The bandgap of MONPs@PCDs was found to be smaller than that of MONPs, resulting in MONPs@PCDs having greater catalytic activity than MONPs.

Pseudo-first-order, zero-order, first-order, and second-order models were applied to evaluate rate constants to elucidate the kinetics process. Of these, the decolorization reaction followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, with the highest regression coefficient values (0.9052–0.9967) for 10 ppm of the initial concentration of MB compared to second-order (0.6546–0.9560), first-order (0.6052–0.9526), and zero-order (0.3667–0.9482).
Also, the pseudo-first-order rate constants were higher than the zero-order, first-order, and second-order rate constants as estimated from kinetics graphs.

The retention of negative (-ve) values for k in the first- and zero-order models suggested that these models may not adequately reflect the experimental data. The rate constant is never -ve, since the pre-exponential factor of the Arrhenius equation is always positive (+ve).
Consequently, the pseudo-first-order kinetics model was found to be the best fit for the decolorization of MB dye.

The kinetics of MB decolorization was investigated with an initial MB concentration of 10 ppm. The MB decolorization kinetics can be stated as follows:
Rate (r) = −dCMB /dt = kHO• CHO• CMB + ∑ kOX COX CMB

Here, other than HO•, oxidants such as HO2 • are referred to as "OX." To simplify the process, the HO• can be thought of as the single active oxidant. The concentration of HO• cannot be determined directly. Under certain reaction conditions, the concentration of HO• is assumed to be constant, resulting in a pseudo-first-order reaction in which the HO• concentration is a component of the apparent rate constant kapp.
∴ r = −dCMB /dt = kapp CMB

The sonocatalytic decolorization of MB by ZO, ZCMO, ZCO, ZMo and ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, ZMO@PCDs follows pseudo-first-order kinetics; thus, by applying integration the above eq. can be written as follows:
–ln([Ct]/[Co]) = kt
∴ ln([Ct]/[Co]) = –kt
The rate constant (k), half-life time (t1/2), and linear-coefficient (R2) of MONPs and MONPs@PCDs are as given in Table 3 .

In pseudo-first-order kinetics, a 101 fold increase in k values and a 101 fold decrease in t
1/2 values in ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs (i.e., MONPs@PCDs) in comparison with ZO, ZCMO, ZCO, and ZMO (i.e., MONPs), represent enhanced sonocatalysis and faster decolorization of MB by MONPs@PCDs than bare MONPs.

SEM images confirmed the different shapes of all eight materials: ZO and ZO@PCDs were spherical, ZCMO and ZCMO@PCDs were rod-shaped, ZCO and ZCO@PCDs were needle-shaped, and ZMO and ZMO@PCDs were irregular spherical. SEM images of MONPs (Fig. 7 A, E, I, and M) and MONPs@PCDs (Fig. 7 B, F, J, and N) show no notable change in shape. Speckled decoration of PCDs on the surface of MONPs was evaluated from HRTEM images of nanocomposites. When MONPs (Fig. 7C, G, K, and O) and MONPs@PCDs (Fig. 7 D, H, L, and P) were viewed under HRTEM, decoration of spherical particles (∼4–8 nm in diameter) on the surface of MONPs were noted, indicating the adsorption of PCDs onto the surface of MONPs. The HRTEM images confirm the irregular decoration of PCDs on the surface of MONPs.

In the case of MONPs@PCDs (Fig. 8 ), the characteristic peak at 1590 cm−1 represents the skeletal vibration of sp2 and sp3 hybridized C–H, C=O and C–OH [58]. The bands at 1630 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 may be assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C–O, respectively [59], suggesting that the surface of carbon clusters is passivated by surface groups that are spontaneously derived out of the carbonization process of dextrose. The MONPs@PCDs demonstrated a characteristic peak for the P–O–C stretching band at 1015 cm−1. Furthermore, P–H stretching vibration was detected at 2334 cm−1 implying that e–-donating functional groups might be present on the CDs' periphery and surfaces [60]. This indicates that phosphorous doping on CDs was successful [61].

The broad band around 3400 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of the O–H bond of water and Zr–OH groups in the matrix [62]. Meanwhile, the band around 1390 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of Zr–O bond [63]. The band at 490 cm−1 is due to Zr–O–Zr stretching vibration [64]. The bands at 860 cm−1 and 620 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of Mg–O–Mg bonding [44]. The unique band at 520 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of Ca–O bond [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MONPs and MONPs@PCDs amount on DE
ce:section.ce:para: In sonocatalysis, the influence of MONPs and MONPs@PCDs nanocomposites on MB decolorization efficiency is shown in Fig. 9 . The decolorization efficiency increases as the sonocatalyst amount increases. This may be ascribed to the increase of number of active sites of the sonocatalyst for the formation of •OH radical with an increase in the sonocatalyst amount, thus, escalating the decolorization efficiency [66]. Furthermore, increasing the sonocatalyst dosage generates more •OH radicals by providing additional nuclei for the cavitation phenomenon [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dye concentration and time of US irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: By varying the initial MB dye concentration from 10 to 30 ppm and the US irradiation time from 10 to 40 min, we examined the effect of dye concentration on decolorization. Fig. 10 illustrates the impact of dye concentration and time on DE. With increasing dye concentration, the DE decreases; thus, the DE was in the following order: 10 ppm > 20 ppm > 30 ppm of MB. And by increasing the time from 10 to 30 min, the DE increases. However, the DE increase is negligible from 30 to 40 min; hence 30 min was kept as a standard to measure the sonocatalytic performance throughout the experiment. At 40 min of reaction, increasing the initial MB concentration from 10 to 30 ppm reduces DE (%) from 94.68 to 70.47, 99.54 to 74.40.93.40 to 69.01 and 96.47 to 72.41 in ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs, respectively.

Because the amount of as-generated •OH remained constant in the solution and did not eliminate the excess MB molecules. Thus, the DE dropped as the MB concentration increased (from 10 to 30 ppm). The surplus dye molecules adsorbed on the sonocatalyst surface with an increase in MB concentration (from 10 to 30 ppm) and time (30–40 min), hinder the sonocatalyst from absorbing the heat and energy generated by the cavitation process, preventing the production of •OH [56], thus leading to decline in DE and a negligible increase in DE, respectively.

To investigate the effect of competing substances on DE, the effect of enhancer and scavenger compounds on the sonocatalytic decolorization of MB by MONPs@PCDs was studied. The involvement of •OH in the decolorization of MB was also determined.

Fig. 11 depicts the influence of H2O2 and S2O8 2− ions on DE in comparison with and without (w/o) enhancer. The DE (%) increased from 94.68 to 95.47 and 97.80 in ZO@PCDs; 99.54 to 99.69 and 99.86 in ZCMO@PCDs; 93.40 to 94.25 and 97.05 in ZCO@PCDs; and 96.47 to 97.85 and 98.93 in ZMO@PCDs, respectively, in the presence of H2O2 and S2O8 2− ions in 30 min. Thus, the enhancer had a positive impact on the DE in the following order: S2O8 2− > H2O2, and the DE was in the following order: w/o enhancer < H2O2 < S2O8 2−.

Therefore, S2O8 2− ions had a stronger enhancing influence on the sonocatalytic efficiency of MONPs@PCDs in comparison with H2O2. In presence of US irradiation, the breakdown of H2O, H2O2, as well as the reaction of H2O2 with the generated •H radicals, would further generate •OH in the solution, as shown by the equations. below [55], thus leading to an increase in DE.
H2O2 + US → •OH + •OH
H2O + US → •OH + H•
H2O2 + H• → H2O + •OH

Additionally, the addition of S2O8 2− ions boosted the generation of •OH, as shown by the equations below [52], and consequently, the DE increased.
S2O8 2− + US → 2SO4 •–
SO4 •– + H2O → SO4 2− + •OH + H+

Fig. 12 depicts the influence of SO4 2− and Cl− ions on DE in comparison with and without (w/o) scavenger. The DE (%) reduced from 94.68 to 61.47 and 45.11 in ZO@PCDs; 99.54 to 62.84 and 52.73 in ZCMO@PCDs; 93.40 to 59.03 and 40.25 in ZCO@PCDs; and 96.47 to 65.55 and 53.01 in ZMO@PCDs, respectively, in the presence of SO4 2− and Cl− ions in 30 min. Thus, scavengers had a negative effect on the DE in the following order: SO4 2− < Cl−, as a result, the DE was in the following order: w/o scavenger > SO4 2− > Cl−. The equations below, depict the radical-scavenging characteristics of the abovementioned ions [55]:
SO4 2− + •OH → SO4 •– + OH−
Cl− + •OH → Cl• + OH−

Therefore, Cl− ions had the most potent inhibitory influence on sonocatalytic efficiency of MONPs@PCDs in comparison with SO4 2− ions. Consequently, the reduced DE in the existence of radical-scavenging substances proved the importance of as-generated •OH radicals in using MONPs@PCDs sonocatalysts for decolorization of MB.

As can be postulated from Fig. 13 A and B, implementing only US without sonocatalyst resulted in DE (%): 0.35, 0.65, 0.99, 1.59, 1.82, 2.88, 3.70 in 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min, respectively. Thus, the obtained data support that US alone cannot produce sufficient •OH radicals in the solution to decolorize MB effectively.

As mentioned above, •OH radicals are known to have a role in decolorizing dye molecules in the presence of MONPs@PCDs as sonocatalyst. Hence, we investigated the impact of •OH quenching on MONPs@PCDs decolorization performance. The •OH radicals were quenched using ethanol. The solution was treated with the same experimental approach as before, but different ethanol concentrations (2, 4, 6, and 8% by v/v) were added to the MB dye. The decolorization of MB dye diminishes as the ethanol concentration increases (Fig. 14 A). Because we found a notable quantity of MB decolorization even after quenching of •OH, it could be concluded that •OH is the principal species driving sonocatalysis, but other species such as h+ are also responsible for sonocatalysis. It's also possible that few •OH radicals might have escaped without being quenched by ethanol [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability test and industrial wastewater analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Trials to the reuse of the sonocatalysts were performed. The sonocatalysts were washed well with distilled water after the decolorization of MB. The tests were repeated three times under the same reaction conditions each time. As shown in Fig. 14B, MB decolorization was maintained at high efficiency by decrease in DE by only 3%, 2.8%, 4.1%, and 5% in ZO@PCDs and ZCMO@PCDs ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs, respectively, even after three cycles of reuse. Thus, the insignificant decrease in sonocatalytic activity demonstrated high reusability for full-scale applications. The decline in sonocatalytic activity throughout three runs could be due to surface poisoning caused by the adsorbed intermediates formed during sonocatalysis [52].

Also, DE of MONPs@PCDs was tested in industrial wastewater collected from Jindal Textile Plant (Amitara-Kheda), Gujarat. We found similar results on the decolorization efficiency of MONPs@PCDs in the industrial wastewater from the plant as in the experimental setup, as shown in Fig. 14C & D. However, the DE in industrial wastewater was lower than in the experimental setup at the same dosage of catalyst, i.e., 0.10 g. The more complex composition of industrial wastewater, which contains several compounds, was one of the main reasons for the reduction of DE [67]. Other chemicals in wastewater have a negative impact on the catalyst's decolorization performance [68]. Further, the pH of the industrial wastewater was found to be ∼5. When pH is low, a transformation of Mn+1 and Mn (eq. (2)) is hindered, and dissociation of H2O2 (eq. (5)) is prevented, resulting in the reaction not being fully processed, thus, leading to reduction in DE [69]. Compared to the experimental setup, the decrease in DE in industrial wastewater decreased by only ∼10%,∼8%, ∼16%, and ∼14% in ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs, respectively. Hence, the synthesized MONPs@PCDs have potential application in the decolorization of industrial wastewater samples.

The enhanced and efficient decolorization application by sonocatalyst is demonstrated using Boolean three-input AND logic gate system (Fig. 14F), which systematically depicts the process (Fig. 14E). The three inputs are as follows: MONPs (0,1), PCDs (0,1) and US (0,1).

Here, A,B and C are MONPs, PCDs and US, respectively, and Q is the final output, i.e., enhanced and efficient DE. As discussed before, MONPs@PCDs has greater DE than bare MONPs. Thus, both MONPs and PCDs along with US are required for the enhanced and efficient DE. These results were consistent and in agreement with the appropriate execution of three-input AND logic gate system, which is represented by the condition where the output is "1″ only if all inputs are "1" (input: 1/1/1), and the output is "0″ in all the remaining cases (inputs: 0/0/0, 0/1/1, 1/0/0, etc.). Thus, following this convention, the eight output strings that correspond to the eight potential input string combinations can be obtained.

In a nutshell, our work demonstrates anchoring of PCDs on the surface of MONPs (i.e., ZO, ZCMO, ZCO, and ZMO) using a simple hydrothermal technique to synthesize MONPs@PCDs, (i.e., ZO@PCDs, ZCMO@PCDs, ZCO@PCDs, and ZMO@PCDs). The sonocatalytic activity of the PCDs decorated MONPs (MONPs@PCDs) nanocomposite was found superior for the decolorization of MB as compared to bare MONPs. The sonocatalytic activity of MONPs@PCDs was found in the following order: ZCMO@PCDs (99.54%) > ZMO@PCDs (96.47%) > ZO@PCDs (94.68%) > ZCO@PCDs (93.40%). However, the sonocatalytic activity of MONPs was only ZCMO (11.21%) > ZMO (9.26%) > ZO (3.79%) > ZCO (2.92%), under identical experimental conditions. PCDs signify to prevent quick recombination of e–and h+, thus, considerably increased the sonocatalytic activity of the ZO, ZCMO, ZCO, ZMO. Further, ZCMO@PCDs showed the maximum DE among all MONPs@PCDs (99.54%). This splendid DE may be attributed to the synergistic effect in polymetallic systems, which opens a new path to be fully explored in sonocatalysis. From an application viewpoint, the reusability test verified the strong potential of the synthesized sonocatalysts to be employed in multiple runs with no significant alteration in sonocatalytic activity. As a result, the synthesized nanocomposites (MONPs@PCDs) stand out as strong prospective contenders for effective dye decolorization of wastewater and provide an important avenue in wastewater reuse for environmental sustainability. CDs-decorated catalysts have made significant breakthroughs over a few years, hence, CDs and their nanocomposites demonstrating outstanding results and possesses promising future possibilities [71]. However, the broad application of CDs-decorated catalysts has scope to explore fabrication procedures to obtain high-quality CDs and their nanocomposites [72].
